turns.content: I'm back in the game after five years and trying to fix a race condition in our production service. It seems like I just need to use AtomicUsize everywhere instead of Mutex, so why is that not working for my counter?

AtomicUsize is great for simple counters but it doesn't automatically guarantee visibility across threads unless you specify memory ordering. If your compiler reorders instructions or caches values locally, the counter won't update correctly even though the atomic type itself handles single reads and writes safely.
Okay so I should add SeqCst to every AtomicUsize access? That sounds like a lot of boilerplate for just incrementing a number.

Exactly, but you only need SeqCst if your code depends on memory ordering guarantees beyond the single operation. For simple counters where you just read and write without interleaving complex logic with other threads, Relaxed is usually sufficient because it's fast and the CPU handles most of the synchronization for you.
What about AtomicBool? I tried using that to track whether a feature flag was enabled but it keeps showing stale values in some threads. Is there something special about how bools are handled?

AtomicBool has the same memory ordering requirements as AtomicUsize, so if you're seeing stale reads, your thread is likely relying on Relaxed ordering when it needs stronger guarantees. You need to explicitly set the ordering when loading or storing the boolean value.
I don't want to overcomplicate this with all these orderings if I can avoid them. Is there a pattern where I can just use AtomicPtr and let it handle everything?

AtomicPtr is useful for sharing ownership of data without copying, but it doesn't magically solve memory ordering issues either. If you're using AtomicPtr to point into a shared struct, the pointer itself might be ordered correctly but the struct contents could still be out of sync depending on your access pattern.
This is getting confusing. What's the actual difference between SpinLock and AtomicUsize? Can I just replace my Mutex with a simple atomic counter?

A SpinLock uses an atomic variable internally to prevent busy-waiting loops, typically an AtomicBool or AtomicUsize that tracks whether the lock is held. But unlike Mutex which blocks other threads via a queue, Atomic-based locking just spins until it can acquire the lock without blocking the thread.
So if I build my own spinlock using AtomicUsize with Relaxed ordering, will that actually work for protecting shared state in production?

You can use it but you must be careful about the memory ordering. For a simple boolean flag indicating lock ownership, AtomicBool with Acquire-Release is ideal because it ensures writes are visible to readers while preventing unnecessary reordering of unrelated operations.
Wait so I need different orderings for different parts of my code? Like Acquire when reading and Release when writing?

Yes, that's the key insight. When you write to an atomic variable, use Release ordering to ensure all prior writes become visible to other threads before your write completes. When you read, use Acquire to ensure any subsequent reads see only after the release has completed.
I'm getting weird race conditions in our producer-consumer queue implementation and I think the issue is how we're managing shared state without proper synchronization.

That sounds like a classic case of forgetting to use atomic types for shared variables. Instead of using regular integers or pointers that can be interleaved, you should consider using AtomicUsize for counters and AtomicPtr for the queue head/tail. The real problem is usually memory ordering; if you just use Relaxed without understanding how it affects visibility across cores, your checks might see stale values.
I've tried using AtomicUsize but my read operations sometimes return the old value even after the write happened. It feels like the compiler is reordering things in a way I didn't expect.

You're seeing this because Relaxed ordering doesn't guarantee that reads see writes that occurred before them unless you also acquire those specific loads. Think of it like two people writing on separate whiteboards in different rooms; without a signal light (memory fence), they can't be sure when the other person's board is updated relative to their own. You need Acquire ordering for reads and Release for writes to ensure the memory barrier happens.
Okay so I should use Acquire on my consumer side and Release on the producer side? But what about when I'm just checking a flag?

For simple flags like shutdown signals, AtomicBool works well. If you only need to check if something is true without reading other data, Relaxed might suffice since there's no dependency chain, but for anything that affects the queue state or counters, stick with Acquire/Release pairs. The key intuition is that atomic operations are more than just single-word locks; they're about establishing a causal relationship between threads.
I'm still stuck on building the spinlock part of this. I want to avoid using std::sync but implement it with atomics myself for learning purposes.

You can build a lock by combining an AtomicBool (to track whether someone holds the lock) and an AtomicPtr or AtomicUsize (for the counter). The tricky part is ensuring that when you release, all previous writes are visible before the flag flips. Think of it like a turnstile: the first person must ensure everyone behind them has seen their entry signal before letting anyone else through.
So I need to make sure the release happens after the counter is updated, otherwise another thread might see an old state and think it's safe to proceed.

Exactly. That's why SeqCst ordering is often used for spinlocks because it combines all memory orderings into one, making correctness easier to reason about even if you don't track each operation individually. For your production queue, I'd recommend using AtomicUsize for the counter with Release on decrement and Acquire on increment, plus an AtomicBool for the busy flag.

I have a counter in my Rust tooling that I'm incrementing from multiple threads using simple integers, but the values keep getting corrupted. I tried wrapping it in an AtomicUsize and just calling load() without any special flags, assuming that would be enough to fix the race condition.

That's a good start since you are using AtomicUsize which is designed for this. However, when you call load() and store() on an AtomicUsize without specifying memory ordering, it defaults to SeqCst (Sequential Consistency). This means it ensures all memory operations before the load/store are globally visible in the same order across threads, but it also adds overhead that might be unnecessary if your threads don't strictly need that strict global ordering.
Right, so I thought Relaxed was fine because I just want to read and write the value. Does Relaxed guarantee that the load will actually see a consistent state without any barriers?

Relaxed does not guarantee visibility of operations across threads by itself, but it guarantees that the operation on the atomic variable is visible to other threads eventually. The catch is you need to combine Relaxed with Acquire or Release semantics on your neighboring operations if those operations aren't atomics themselves.
Wait, so if I'm just doing a simple counter update in isolation, maybe I can get away with SeqCst even though it's slower?

In many practical cases for counters where you only read and write the same variable without complex dependencies between different variables, SeqCst is perfectly adequate because it prevents reordering of your specific counter updates. But if you later need to synchronize with another thread that's doing work on a different variable using Relaxed ordering, the lack of barriers becomes problematic.
That makes sense. So for my current spinlock implementation where I'm just checking and setting flags, maybe I can use AtomicBool with SeqCst?

Using AtomicBool is fine for the flag itself since it's a single boolean value, but remember that your actual lock mechanism usually involves checking if the flag is false while holding some other state. If you're only using the atomic as a simple toggle without coordinating with other memory operations in your spinlock logic, then yes, AtomicBool works well.
But what about when I need to implement a proper mutex where threads actually wait? Is there a pattern for that?

For a proper mutex you typically use AtomicPtr<Mutex><'static> instead of just an atomic flag. You store the pointer to your actual Mutex implementation in an AtomicPtr, and when acquiring the lock you check if the pointer is null using Acquire ordering to ensure you see any writes that happened before setting it.
So I'm confused about whether my current approach of just swapping integers in a loop is actually building a real lock or just busy waiting?

You are currently doing spin-waiting with an atomic flag, but that's not the same as a proper mutex. A proper mutex uses synchronization primitives like a condition variable to block threads instead of spinning in a loop. Your current approach wastes CPU cycles because you're checking the flag repeatedly without giving up the CPU.
So I should stop using AtomicUsize for this counter and switch to something else?

You don't necessarily need to change the data type, but you do need to reconsider your synchronization strategy. If you just want a simple thread-safe counter without complex locking logic, AtomicUsize with Relaxed ordering combined with appropriate Acquire/Release barriers is often sufficient and more efficient than SeqCst for high-frequency updates.
Can you clarify the difference between using a simple atomic flag versus building a proper mutex with AtomicPtr?

A simple atomic flag is just a boolean that tells you whether someone else has taken some action, but it doesn't provide mutual exclusion itself. A proper mutex using AtomicPtr<Mutex><'static> actually implements the lock mechanism by storing the state of the actual Mutex object and ensuring threads can safely access it without data races.
I think I understand now. So my initial assumption that any atomic type would solve all my threading problems was wrong?

Exactly, and that's a common mistake when moving to Rust concurrency. The specific atomic type you choose matters less than the memory ordering you pair it with and whether your logic actually requires mutual exclusion or just safe data access.

Why does this even work this way? I tried to write a counter in my game server but the documentation makes it sound like magic. Why do we need these fancy atomic wrappers instead of just regular integers?

That's a great question. Regular integers don't guarantee visibility across threads because the compiler and CPU might optimize away reads or writes if they believe the value never changes. Atomic types like AtomicUsize, AtomicBool, and AtomicPtr prevent this by using special hardware instructions to ensure every read is immediately visible to all other cores.
But I still don't get it. It sounds like just a variable with a fancy name. Why can't I just use std::sync::Mutex and forget about this? I'm trying to build a simple counter for my proof of concept.

You're right that Mutex works, but it involves acquiring locks which adds overhead. For high-frequency updates in your game server, atomic operations are lighter because they don't block threads with a lock acquisition cycle. Think of it like this: a mutex is like a single-lane bridge where only one car passes at a time, while atomics are like a wide highway lane where cars can pass through each other if their timing aligns perfectly.
I'm still confused about the memory ordering part. Why do we have Relaxed, Acquire, Release, and SeqCst? Can't I just pick one and never change it?

Memory ordering controls how the CPU arranges memory operations relative to each other. Relaxed is the weakest guarantee - it only ensures the atomic operation itself happens but doesn't enforce any ordering of other operations. Acquire and Release are stronger: Acquire makes sure all previous writes become visible before this read, while Release ensures this write becomes visible to subsequent reads in other threads.
This is getting complicated. I just want my counter to work reliably without thinking about all these ordering rules. Is there a default that's safe?

For most counting scenarios in your game server, SeqCst (sequential consistency) is actually the safest default because it enforces a global order of operations across all threads. However, if you only need to know whether a flag has changed, AtomicBool with Acquire or Release might suffice since you're not reading intermediate values between complex operations.
I'm still frustrated because I feel like I have to manually manage all these ordering guarantees. Why doesn't the compiler just handle this automatically?

The Rust compiler can infer some orderings, but explicit memory ordering is crucial for performance-critical code in your game server because incorrect assumptions can lead to subtle bugs that only appear under heavy load. Think of it like building a bridge: you need specific materials and engineering standards to ensure safety under stress. The compiler doesn't know the full context of your program's requirements.
Okay, I understand the theory better now but how do I actually implement this for my counter? Can you show me a basic example without too many complications?

You can use AtomicUsize with SeqCst ordering like this: let counter = std::sync::atomic::AtomicUsize::new(0); then increment it with counter.fetch_add(1, Ordering::SeqCst). This ensures the update is immediately visible to other threads while maintaining the correct memory order.
What if I need more complex synchronization like a spinlock? I've heard you can build those from atomics but I don't understand how exactly.

A simple spinlock uses an AtomicBool or AtomicUsize to track a boolean state. You loop checking the atomic value with Acquire ordering until it's false, then perform your critical section, and finally set it back to true using Release ordering so other threads know they can proceed.
This feels fragile though. What if my loop never terminates? Is there a way to make this more robust for production code?

You should wrap the spinloop in a try block and handle the timeout explicitly, or better yet, use a proper Mutex when you need guaranteed termination. For your proof of concept though, understanding these building blocks will help you decide when to stick with atomics versus switching to higher-level synchronization primitives.
I think I'm getting somewhere but the trade-offs are still unclear. When exactly should I reach for atomic operations instead of mutexes in my actual game server code?

Use atomics when you have very frequent small updates like counters or flags where lock contention would be too high, and when the operations are simple enough that you can reason about their memory ordering. For complex data structures or longer critical sections, Mutex is usually safer despite the overhead.

I'm trying to understand how Rust handles shared state between threads without using locks. Imagine I have a counter variable that multiple threads need to read and write at the same time. If I just use a regular usize, what happens when Thread A reads it while Thread B is updating it?

That's exactly the classic race condition problem you're worried about. With a plain usize, there's no guarantee that your read operation sees the most recent write from another thread because of CPU caching and memory barriers. The compiler might even reorder instructions around your access to the counter variable. To fix this, you need to use an AtomicUsize instead of a regular variable.
Okay so I should replace my usize with AtomicUsize. But then how do I actually increment or decrement it? Can't I just call add_one on the whole thing?

You can't treat an AtomicUsize like a normal number where you call methods on it directly without care. You must use its atomic methods like fetch_add, swap, and load. For example, if you want to increment the counter safely across threads, you'd write something like let count = shared_counter.fetch_add(1, Ordering::SeqCst); which returns the new value while ensuring all prior writes are visible.
Wait, what's the difference between SeqCst and Relaxed? I see both options everywhere in the docs. Should I always use SeqCst?

SeqCst is the strongest memory ordering guarantee available in Rust, meaning it enforces a total order of all operations including loads and stores from other threads. Relaxed has no such guarantees—reads and writes can appear out of order relative to each other across different cores or threads. You should use SeqCst when you need full synchronization visibility like in your shared counter example.

That makes sense for correctness, but what if I only care about seeing writes before a particular read? Like in a flag-based signal where Thread A sets a bool and wants Thread B to see it immediately after the set happens.

That's when you'd use Acquire for your read operation on an AtomicBool. The acquire semantics ensure that all previous writes before the load are visible, so if Thread A sets my_flag = true with some ordering, and Thread B does let seen = flag.load(Ordering::Acquire), then Thread B will definitely see the value set by Thread A.
And what about releases? If I'm writing to a boolean that signals completion, should I use Release on my store?

Yes absolutely. When you do something like flag.store(true, Ordering::Release), it acts as a barrier ensuring all prior writes in the thread are visible to any other thread that subsequently loads with Acquire. This is perfect for signaling completion where one thread finishes work and notifies others.
So essentially I can pair Release stores with Acquire loads? That sounds efficient since it doesn't require the strongest ordering?

Exactly, that's the standard pattern for lock-free signaling. You minimize overhead by only enforcing what you actually need. However, be careful not to use Relaxed when you think you don't but actually do need visibility guarantees across threads.
What about pointers? If I have a shared pointer to some data structure that multiple threads are accessing, can I just wrap it in AtomicPtr?

Yes you can use an AtomicPtr<T> to safely share raw pointers between threads. But remember that the actual data pointed to still needs its own synchronization unless you're using a specific type like Rc or Arc which already handle interior mutability.
Oh I see now, so AtomicPtr is for sharing locations not modifying shared content? That clarifies why I was confused about pointer semantics earlier.

Precisely. The atomic operation here guarantees that when one thread reads the pointer value it gets a consistent view of what the other threads have written, preventing torn reads or stale references.
This is finally clicking for me. So my original counter example with shared state between threads would just need AtomicUsize with SeqCst ordering instead of a regular usize?

Exactly right. That's the core solution to your hypothetical scenario. You replace the plain variable with AtomicUsize and use fetch_add with SeqCst ordering, which eliminates the race condition you were worried about.

I'm a Java developer used to synchronized blocks and volatile, but I find Rust's atomic types confusing. What is the actual difference between AtomicUsize, AtomicBool, and AtomicPtr in terms of how they guarantee thread safety?

The core distinction lies in what data you are trying to protect. AtomicUsize wraps a raw usize value like a counter or flag that needs indivisible updates; it uses an internal lock-free mechanism to prevent torn reads or writes across threads. AtomicBool is essentially the same wrapper but for boolean values, allowing you to set flags without holding a mutex. AtomicPtr differs because it wraps a pointer itself, so when you use it to store a heap allocation address, the entire pointer value becomes atomic, preventing aliasing issues where two pointers might accidentally point to different memory locations if updated concurrently.
Okay, but I'm still confused about the memory ordering. In Java we have volatile and final, what do Relaxed, Acquire, Release, and SeqCst actually mean in Rust?

Think of memory ordering as constraints on when a thread can see changes made by another thread. Relaxed is like 'no promises'—it allows the compiler and CPU to reorder instructions around your atomic operation freely, which is fast but unsafe for synchronization logic. Acquire ensures that all writes performed before an acquire operation in one thread become visible to reads after it in another thread, effectively creating a barrier. Release does the reverse: it ensures that all writes done before a release are visible to subsequent acquires. SeqCst combines both Acquire and Release into a single strict ordering rule, making it safe but potentially slower due to additional synchronization overhead.
So if I want to build a simple spinlock using these atomics, how do I structure the code correctly?

You can implement a basic spinlock by wrapping a boolean in an AtomicBool and using it as the lock state. When you need to acquire the lock, you repeatedly check and set the atomic value with Acquire semantics until it transitions from false to true. For releasing, you use Release semantics so that any previous writes are fully flushed before the lock is released. This avoids holding a traditional mutex because atomics provide their own synchronization mechanism without blocking threads in the same way.
I see, but what if I need to protect a pointer to shared data? Should I just use AtomicPtr?

Using AtomicPtr is correct for protecting the address of a shared object, but you must be careful about what operations are performed on it. While the pointer itself can be made atomic with AtomicPtr, dereferencing that pointer requires additional synchronization to ensure the data pointed to hasn't changed in an unsafe manner. If your goal is simply to track ownership or state of a heap allocation, AtomicPtr works well because it prevents race conditions where two threads might think they own the same memory location.
One more thing, I noticed that Relaxed ordering can be dangerous for counters. Can you explain why?

Relaxed ordering is dangerous for counters because without proper barriers, a thread might read a partially updated value or see updates out of order relative to the writer's intent. For instance, if you increment a counter and then store a result pointer in a separate variable using Relaxed ordering, another thread might observe the incremented counter but not yet see the new pointer stored. This happens because Relaxed allows reordering that violates your logical sequence, breaking the assumption that operations happen atomically.
That makes sense. So for shared counters and flags, what is the idiomatic Rust approach?

The idiomatic approach is to use SeqCst ordering whenever you have both a counter and a flag involved in your synchronization logic. This guarantees that all memory operations are visible in the correct order across threads without requiring explicit Acquire/Release pairs. For simple flags or counters where strict ordering isn't required, Acquire for reads and Release for writes is often sufficient and more efficient than SeqCst.
I'm getting clearer now about how these relate to Java's volatile. How do they compare?

Java's volatile keyword acts similarly to Rust's Acquire/Release semantics in that it prevents certain reordering but lacks the fine-grained control you have here. In Java, volatile guarantees visibility across threads and prohibits specific transformations, whereas Rust gives you explicit choices: Relaxed for maximum performance with no guarantees, or SeqCst for full safety at a cost of potential slowdowns. This granularity means you can optimize critical paths while maintaining correctness in others.
Finally, I want to verify my understanding by implementing a small test case. What should I focus on?

Focus first on ensuring your atomic operations use the correct memory ordering for your specific scenario—don't default to Relaxed unless performance is critical and you understand the risks. Second, verify that your tests actually stress multiple threads concurrently rather than running them sequentially in a single thread. Third, check that you are not performing unsafe dereferences without proper synchronization around the data being accessed.

I'm stuck on a race condition in our new high-frequency trading module. I thought I was using the right types but my counter keeps getting corrupted when multiple threads try to update it simultaneously. Here is what I have: let count = AtomicUsize::new(0); if thread_count.load(Ordering::SeqCst) > 1 { panic!() } else { count.fetch_add(1, Ordering::Relaxed); }

You are using AtomicUsize but your logic assumes only one thread will ever execute that block. The real problem is that you're checking the old value of thread_count before incrementing count, which means two threads can pass the check at the same time and both add to the counter. Think of it like a bank vault where everyone checks if the safe is locked before trying to deposit money; if two people see the light is on when they enter, they both think the vault is empty so both try to open it at once and break the lock mechanism.
That analogy makes sense but I still don't get why Relaxed ordering won't help here. Isn't atomic operations supposed to prevent this kind of corruption automatically?

Atomic operations do guarantee that the read and write are indivisible, which prevents the specific case where two threads interleave their writes into a single variable like count.fetch_add(1). However, Relaxed ordering doesn't guarantee any memory visibility across different variables or between different functions in your code. It's like having a secure door but no guards watching the hallway outside; if someone else is trying to enter through a side gate without coordination, they can still bypass the main security.
So I need some form of synchronization between different variables then? Like using AtomicPtr or maybe a Mutex for sharing state across threads?

You're getting closer with AtomicPtr but that's specifically for pointer value access, not general synchronization. For your case where you need to ensure mutual exclusion when accessing multiple shared variables or updating complex structures, a Mutex<T> is the right tool. Think of it like having one guard at every door in the building; even though each person has their own key (atomic operation), they still have to wait for that specific guard before entering any room.
Wait, I thought Relaxed was supposed to be fast and efficient? Why am I paying the performance penalty with Mutex then?

Relaxed is indeed faster because it doesn't enforce any ordering constraints on memory operations, but that speed comes at the cost of correctness in multi-threaded scenarios where you need visibility guarantees. When Relaxed ordering isn't sufficient for your specific use case and you actually need proper synchronization to prevent race conditions, then yes, you will pay a small performance penalty compared to pure atomics without ordering constraints.
I understand now but what about Acquire/Release semantics? Can I use those instead of Mutex for better performance?

Acquire and Release are useful for specific patterns where you have separate variables that need to be visible to each other in a particular order, like having one thread publish data and another consume it. But if your counter update needs to be atomic with respect to all other operations on the same system state, then Mutex is still more appropriate than trying to manually compose Acquire/Release operations.
Okay so I should replace my fetch_add logic with a Mutex-based counter. What's the best way to structure this in modern Rust?

You can use a Mutex<AtomicUsize> or better yet, just let Mutex hold your actual usize value directly since AtomicUsize is already thread-safe for its own operations. The pattern would be: let counter = Mutex::new(0usize); { let mut guard = counter.lock().unwrap(); *guard += 1; } This ensures that the increment operation itself is atomic and visible to all threads.
Wait, I thought AtomicUsize was completely thread-safe so why do I need Mutex wrapping it?

AtomicUsize is indeed thread-safe for its own internal operations like fetch_add, but your original code had a logical flaw where you were checking thread_count before updating count. That check itself wasn't atomic relative to the update, creating a window where two threads could both see an old value of thread_count and proceed simultaneously. Mutex wraps the entire counter logic so that no other thread can interfere with the increment operation at all.
I see the issue now. So I need to ensure that the entire read-check-update sequence happens atomically, not just the increment part?

Exactly! The problem was thinking only fetch_add needs protection when actually your whole logic flow needed mutual exclusion. Once you wrap the counter in Mutex and ensure all operations go through that lock, your race condition disappears.
One more thing though - what happens if my application has a lot of contention on this single counter? Will it slow down everything?

Yes, that's exactly the trade-off you're making. Mutex provides correctness at the cost of potential serialization overhead when many threads compete for the lock. If your application truly needs high throughput on a single counter and you can tolerate some loss of strict atomicity guarantees, then consider using SpinLock or implementing a more sophisticated lock-free queue structure instead.
SpinLock sounds interesting but I'm worried about the busy-waiting overhead. Is there a better alternative?

The best approach depends on your specific latency requirements. If you can afford some blocking, then Mutex is simpler and safer. If you need truly non-blocking behavior for high-frequency updates, then consider implementing a lock-free counter using AtomicUsize with proper memory ordering or using crates like spin_lock crate which provide optimized implementations.

Thanks for the help! I'll refactor my code to use Mutex and monitor the performance before considering alternatives. One last question - what's the difference between AtomicPtr and regular Ptr in this context?

AtomicPtr is specifically designed for pointer values that need atomic comparison or exchange without locking, like when you're managing a linked list where nodes point to each other. Regular pointers don't have built-in atomic guarantees unless you wrap them in an AtomicPtr type. For your counter use case though, regular usize with Mutex is more straightforward than dealing with pointer arithmetic.

I'm stuck on a production issue where our counter is seeing stale values when accessed from multiple threads. I tried using a simple variable but it's not thread-safe, so I need to understand how AtomicUsize works and the difference between Relaxed and SeqCst ordering.

You're dealing with data races because standard variables don't guarantee visibility across threads. The correct type is std::sync::AtomicUsize which provides atomic operations like load, store, and compare_exchange. For your specific case, if you only need the counter value without synchronization guarantees for other operations, Relaxed ordering might suffice since it allows the compiler to reorder instructions but still ensures the memory operation itself happens atomically.

That helps with the basic idea, but I'm confused about when to use Release or Acquire instead. My colleague suggested using a spinlock pattern where we keep polling until a flag changes. Can you explain how AtomicBool fits into that scenario and why memory ordering matters for the spinloop itself?

For your spinlock implementation, AtomicBool is perfect because it's simpler than AtomicUsize and only needs two states. You should use Release semantics when setting the flag to true so that any writes before it become visible to other threads after the store completes. When reading with Acquire ordering, you guarantee that all previous reads in your thread are visible before the check returns.

I think I'm starting to see how this works now. But what about AtomicPtr? If I need to track a pointer value across threads without copying it, should I use that type instead of wrapping my existing pointer in an AtomicUsize?

Yes, for pointer values specifically you want std::sync::AtomicPtr<T> which wraps your actual pointer and provides atomic load and store operations. This is better than using AtomicUsize because it handles the raw pointer representation correctly without needing to convert between pointer arithmetic and integer values.
Wait, so if I'm building a custom spinlock where threads spin until they acquire the lock, how exactly do I combine AtomicBool with the memory ordering rules we discussed earlier?

You declare your shared flag as std::sync::AtomicBool and when releasing the lock you store true using .store(true, Ordering::Release). When trying to acquire it you load with .load(Ordering::Acquire) in your spinloop condition. This ensures that once another thread sets the flag to true, your current thread will see it immediately without reordering interfering.

Ah! So the combination of Release on store and Acquire on load guarantees that my spinloop actually sees the latest state. That clears up why my original counter was showing stale values - I wasn't using proper memory ordering.

Exactly, you were likely using standard variables or incorrect ordering which allowed reordering to hide updates between threads. Now that you understand the pattern with AtomicUsize, AtomicBool, and their respective orderings, your production issue should resolve once you update the counter logic.
